title: Module 2 quiz: Prompt engineering for Claude

questions.prompt: Why does Anthropic specifically recommend XML tags?

choices.text: Claude is trained on tagged prompts. Tags delimit sections cleanly, let instructions reference content by name, and harden against prompt injection because tagged content reads as data, not instructions.

questions.explanation: XML tags are a trained behavior on Claude — Anthropic uses them throughout its docs and training. The cleanliness, referenceability, and injection-resistance compound to make tags the single highest-ROI prompt change you can make.

questions.prompt: Your intent classifier is at 68% with a description-only prompt. Best next move?

choices.text: Add 3–5 tagged few-shot examples covering the input distribution including edge cases. Show output exactly as you want it. Typical jump: 15–30 accuracy points with no model change.

questions.explanation: Few-shot is the canonical accuracy boost for classification on Claude. Cover the distribution, include edge cases, wrap each example in <example>/<input>/<output>. Cache the block once stable to control cost.

questions.prompt: Your JSON parser breaks because Claude occasionally wraps responses in markdown fences. Canonical fix?

choices.text: Prefill the assistant message with `{` so Claude continues from there. Preamble disappears; markdown fences vanish; parse failures drop to zero. Combine with few-shot for full reliability.

questions.explanation: Prefilling is Claude's built-in 'lock the response format' feature. Adding `{ role: 'assistant', content: '{' }` as the last message starts Claude's response mid-document, eliminating preamble and formatting drift.

questions.prompt: You want to extract just a French translation, no commentary. What's the right pattern?

choices.text: Prefill `<french>` and set `stop_sequences: ['</french>']`. Claude produces only the translation and stops cleanly. Bonus: latency drops because there's less to generate.
Increase max_tokens and trust the model to be terse.

questions.explanation: Prefilling + stop sequences is the standard pattern for extracting a single field cleanly. The opening tag locks where the response starts; the stop sequence prevents any post-content.

choices.text: On every task — always add 'think step by step.'

On multi-step reasoning, math, logic, complex judgment, and agent planning. Skip CoT on lookups, classification, simple extraction — it just wastes tokens. Use structured CoT (`<thinking>`/`<answer>` tags) so the output stays parseable.

questions.explanation: CoT pays off when the task is genuinely multi-step. Forced CoT on simple tasks adds latency and cost without value. The reliable pattern: structured CoT in tags, demonstrated via few-shot, prefilled to guarantee the format.
